prs.title: Add reusable sidebar navigation
prs.body:
It adds a reusable sidebar navigation pattern and wires it into the learning resources area.

What changed:
- Added a new reusable sidebar component in project/src/components/Sidebar.tsx.
- The sidebar supports category navigation, active-state highlighting, item counts, and a mobile toggle so it collapses cleanly on smaller screens.
- Updated project/src/components/SearchableResources.tsx so resources can now be filtered by category as well as keyword search.
- Exported the new component from project/src/components/index.ts.
- Included the generated root package-lock.json from the workspace install.

Behavior impact:
- Users can now click categories in the sidebar to narrow the resource list.
- The selected category stays visually active.
- On mobile, the sidebar can be expanded and collapsed instead of always taking up space.
- Search and category filtering work together, so the resource list is more focused and reusable for future sections.

Validation:
- I ran `npm run lint` successfully before the push.
- closes #14

prs.title: Add searchable resources and data
prs.body:
# Description

Adds client-side search for roadmaps and topics, a small data source of resources, and replaces hardcoded resource cards with a reusable, searchable component.

## Changes Made

- **Added:** resources.ts — sample `Resource` data and type.
- **Added:** SearchableResources.tsx — client search UI that filters by title, description, and tags, shows result count and empty state, and renders `ResourceCard`.
- **Updated:** index.ts — export `SearchableResources`.
- **Updated:** page.tsx — replaced hardcoded `ResourceCard` entries with `SearchableResources`.

## Related Issue

Closes #16 

## Checklist

- [ ] **Code follows project standards:** code style consistent with repo.
- [ ] **Documentation updated:** describe the new component/data (optional README or comments).
- [ ] **Tested locally in this format:** ran dev server and validated search UI (run `npm run dev`).

prs.title: added reusable cards
prs.body:
**Title:** Add Discord CTA, remove starter content, and add reusable ResourceCard

**Summary:**  
- Replaces the default Next.js starter content with a focused homepage for Demon Tech Roadmap.  
- Adds a prominent “Join our Discord” CTA (invite: https://discord.gg/yWtjK2Tb8T) to encourage community participation.  
- Adds a reusable `ResourceCard` component and a “Learning Resources” grid with sample resources.

**Files changed:**  
- **Page:** page.tsx — removed boilerplate, added project header, Discord CTA, and resources grid.  
- **Component:** ResourceCard.tsx — new reusable card component (props: `title`, `description`, `href`, `tags`).  
- **Exports:** index.ts — re-exports the component for easy imports.

**Why:**  
- Improves first impressions by replacing generic starter text with project context and a clear next step (join community).  
- Provides curated starting resources and a reusable card component to scale resource lists consistently.
#19  resolved

prs.title: Fix navbar overlapping hero section and resolve hydration mismatch error
prs.body:
1. Fixed Navbar Overlapping Hero Section:
Problem: The fixed Navbar was covering the top portion of the Hero slider because it wasn't occupying space in the document flow.
Solution: Added responsive top padding (pt-[8vh], sm:pt-[10vh], lg:pt-[16vh], 2xl:pt-[11vh]) to the main-container in 
src/app/page.js
. This creates exactly enough space for the Navbar's height on all screen sizes.
UX Improvement: Changed the main container height from h-screen to min-h-screen to ensure smooth scrolling as requested ("keep it floating").


2. Resolved Hydration Mismatch Error:
Problem: A "Hydration failed" error was appearing due to browser extensions adding attributes to the <html> tag and slight mismatches in font variable class names on the <body> tag.
Solution: Added suppressHydrationWarning={true} to both the <html> and <body> tags in
src/app/layout.js
. This is the standard fix to prevent development-time hydration warnings caused by client-side modifications that occur before React takes over.

prs.title: Feat/schedule recording filter
prs.body:
Description
The legacy upstream system allowed attendees to filter the public schedule based on whether sessions would be recorded or not. This functionality was missing from the current implementation.

This PR reintroduces the recording filter as a compact cam icon button in the schedule filter bar, keeping the interface clean while making it easily discoverable.

Changes
Recording Filter UI
Added a cam icon button to the filter bar alongside existing controls (track, favorites, timezone).
Clicking the icon opens a small dropdown with three options:
All sessions (default)
Recorded only
Not recorded
When a filter other than "All sessions" is active, the icon displays a clear active state with a primary-colored border and subtle background tint.
The dropdown closes automatically when clicking outside of it.
Visibility Condition
The recording filter is only displayed when:

There is at least one session marked as recorded, and
There is at least one session marked as not recorded.
If all sessions share the same recording status, the cam icon is hidden entirely to avoid unnecessary UI clutter.

Functional Requirements
Works across grid view, list view, and the public-facing schedule widget.
Combines correctly with all existing filters (track, favorites, timezone, day selection).
Schedule updates dynamically without a full page reload.
Filter state is stored in the URL query parameter (?recording=yes or ?recording=no), enabling shareable filtered URLs and persistence on reload.
Included in the "clear all filters" reset action.
Accessible via aria-label and title attributes on the filter button.
Files Changed

app/eventyay/webapp/src/views/schedule/index.vue
 — Recording filter for the main webapp schedule (grid and list views).

app/eventyay/webapp/src/views/schedule/sessions/index.vue
 — Recording filter for the sessions list view.

app/eventyay/static/agenda/js/pretalx-schedule.min.js
 — Recording filter for the public-facing pretalx-schedule widget.
Technical Notes
No backend changes are required. The filter leverages the existing do_not_record field already exposed in the schedule JSON data via Schedule.build_data().
The 

Session.vue
 component already renders a "do not record" icon on individual session cards. This filter complements that existing visual indicator at the schedule level.

Resolved #2614

prs.title: Fix/talks i18n missing translations
prs.body: ## Summary This PR fixes a localization completeness issue in the Talks component where several user-facing strings were permanently stuck in English regardless of the active locale. The problem affected both the Mail Settings UI and all predefined email message templates (acceptance, rejection, confirmation, schedule notifications, etc.). Additionally, a critical bug was found where the wrong email template was being sent to speakers for new schedule notifications. All fixes are backward-compatible — no msgids were changed, no database migrations are required, and all existing [.po](cci:7://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/locale/cs/LC_MESSAGES/django.po:0:0-0:0) translations remain valid. --- ## Problem Statement ### 🔴 Issue 1 — Mail signature help text not translating The `signature` field in [MailSettingsForm](cci:2://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/orga/forms/event.py:181:0-306:9) displayed a help text: > *"The signature will be added to outgoing mails, preceded by "-- ". > You can use Markdown here."* This string was permanently stuck in English regardless of the user's locale. It did not appear to be extractable for translation via Weblate. ### 🔴 Issue 2 — "You can use Markdown here." phrase always in English The shared `phrases.base.use_markdown` phrase — used across mail settings, review forms, submission fields, schedule fields, CfP fields, and more — was always rendered in English. This affected every form in the application that referenced this phrase. ### 🔴 Issue 3 — ALL Talks message templates broken (critical) All predefined message templates in the Talks component (acceptance emails, rejection emails, schedule notifications) were not translating correctly. Furthermore, speakers were silently receiving **incorrect email content** for new schedule release notifications — they were getting an organiser-internal "a talk has been modified" message instead of the intended "new schedule published" message, in every language. --- ## Root Cause Analysis All three issues share the same underlying Django anti-pattern: evaluating lazy translation strings too early (at module/class import time rather than at request render time). ### Root Cause 1 & 2 — Eager evaluation of lazy strings **[app/eventyay/common/text/phrases.py](cci:7://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/common/text/phrases.py:0:0-0:0)** ```python # BROKEN — .format() forces evaluation of the lazy string at class-body level # (module import time). The result is a plain str, not a lazy proxy. # It resolves in whichever language is active at startup — always English. use_markdown = _('You can use {link_start}Markdown{link_end} here.').format( link_start='', link_end='', ) # BROKEN — str() immediately resolves the lazy proxy to a plain str. # The concatenation then produces a non-translatable string literal. help_text=str(_('The signature will be added to outgoing mails, preceded by "-- ".')) + ' ' + phrases.base.use_markdown # also already evaluated (see above) Root Cause 3 — Duplicate variable names (silent overwrite) app/eventyay/mail/default_templates.py python # First definition — correct: speaker-facing new schedule notification UPDATE_SUBJECT = LazyI18nString.from_gettext(_('New schedule!')) UPDATE_TEXT = LazyI18nString.from_gettext(_("""Hi! We have released a new schedule version...""")) # ... 40 lines later ... # Second definition — silently OVERWRITES the first binding. # Python replaces UPDATE_SUBJECT and UPDATE_TEXT with the organiser-internal # "talk modified" notification, never raising any error or warning. UPDATE_SUBJECT = LazyI18nString.from_gettext(_('[{event_name}] A talk has been modified...')) UPDATE_TEXT = LazyI18nString.from_gettext(_("""Hi, the proposal has been modified...""")) The NEW_SCHEDULE role in get_default_template() then resolved to the wrong pair, meaning every speaker receiving a "new schedule" email got the internal organiser notification body instead — in every language. Changes 1. app/eventyay/common/text/phrases.py Added import: from django.utils.text import format_lazy Fixed use_markdown: Replaced the eager .format() call with format_lazy(). The lazy string is now resolved at request render time in the active user locale. The original msgid ('You can use {link_start}Markdown{link_end} here.') is preserved exactly — all existing .po translations remain valid. python # BEFORE use_markdown = _('You can use {link_start}Markdown{link_end} here.').format( link_start='', link_end='', ) # AFTER use_markdown = format_lazy( _('You can use {link_start}Markdown{link_end} here.'), link_start='', link_end='', ) 2. app/eventyay/orga/forms/event.py Fixed signature field help_text: Replaced str() wrapping and plain string concatenation with format_lazy(). Both the description sentence and the Markdown help link are now evaluated at render time. python # BEFORE help_text=str(_('The signature will be added to outgoing mails, preceded by "-- ".')) + ' ' + phrases.base.use_markdown # AFTER help_text=format_lazy( '{description} {markdown}', description=_('The signature will be added to outgoing mails, preceded by "-- ".'), markdown=phrases.base.use_markdown, ) 3. app/eventyay/mail/default_templates.py Renamed duplicate variables: The second UPDATE_SUBJECT / UPDATE_TEXT pair (organiser notification for a modified talk) renamed to MODIFIED_SUBJECT / MODIFIED_TEXT with a clarifying comment. MailTemplateRoles.NEW_SCHEDULE now correctly resolves to the speaker-facing UPDATE_SUBJECT / UPDATE_TEXT (new schedule notification). All msgids unchanged — existing translations remain valid. python # BEFORE (collision) UPDATE_SUBJECT = LazyI18nString.from_gettext(_('New schedule!')) # line 53 # ... UPDATE_SUBJECT = LazyI18nString.from_gettext(_('[...] A talk has been modified...')) # line 102 — OVERWRITES # AFTER (clear distinction) UPDATE_SUBJECT = LazyI18nString.from_gettext(_('New schedule!')) # ... # This template notifies organisers when a speaker modifies their proposal. MODIFIED_SUBJECT = LazyI18nString.from_gettext(_('[...] A talk has been modified...')) RESOLVE #2550 ## Summary by Sourcery Improve mobile navigation layout and fix localization and email template issues in the talks/mail settings area. Bug Fixes: - Ensure the shared 'use Markdown' help text and mail signature help text are properly lazily translated and respect the active locale. - Correct the talks email template configuration so the organiser-only "talk modified" template no longer overrides the speaker-facing new schedule notification. Enhancements: - Refine mobile navbar layout to prevent overflow, truncation, and wrapping issues on small screens, including more compact admin/user controls. - Improve sidebar behavior and visuals on mobile by turning it into a fixed slide-in panel with a dimmed page backdrop and adjusted content spacing. - Adjust common form label styling to better align labels in horizontal forms and avoid layout issues across viewports.

prs.title: Fix/mobile nav responsive UI
prs.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes a series of mobile-specific UI regressions affecting the top
navigation bar and left sidebar across multiple pages of the eventyay platform.
Elements were misaligned, overlapping, or overflowing on devices with a screen
width ≤ 768 px. Desktop behaviour is completely unaffected — all changes are
scoped strictly inside mobile-only media queries.

---

## Problem Statement

Users on mobile devices (phones) reported the following issues with screenshots:

### 🔴 Issue 1 — Navbar overflow / misalignment (Event start page & admin area)
The fixed top navbar (50 px tall) contained multiple competing elements —
site brand/name, search bar, "Admin mode" button, user profile link, and globe
icon — with no flex constraints on mobile. On narrow viewports (~375 px), these
items overflowed the navbar, pushed each other off-screen, or collapsed in an
inconsistent and confusing layout.

### 🔴 Issue 2 — Sidebar content bleed-through (Side menu context)
When the left sidebar was opened on mobile, the underlying page content (text
labels, navigation links) was visually bleeding through the sidebar panel. This
created an unreadable mess where sidebar items and page content text overlapped.

Root causes:
- In the **orga/talk** area: sidebar used `position: absolute` instead of
  `fixed`, meaning it scrolled incorrectly; no opaque background guaranteed;
  no overlay/backdrop to visually separate it from page content.
- In the **pretixcontrol** area: sidebar `z-index` was set to `12` — far too
  low to sit above Bootstrap dropdowns (`z=1000`), allowing page content to
  paint over sidebar items.

### 🔴 Issue 3 — Page content overlapping the navbar (Orga event page)
On the talk/orga event detail page, the main `#page-content` block's mobile
override was accidentally removing the `margin-top: 50px` that keeps content
below the fixed 50 px navbar. As a result, page titles and content started
rendering directly beneath (and behind) the navbar bar.

---

## Root Cause Analysis

| File | Root Cause |
|------|-----------|
| [_layout.css](cci:7://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/static/orga/css/_layout.css:0:0-0:0) | Mobile `@media` block overrode `#page-content` without restoring `margin-top: 50px`; sidebar used `position: absolute`; no flex constraints on navbar |
| [_sidebar.scss](cci:7://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/static/pretixcontrol/scss/_sidebar.scss:0:0-0:0) | Sidebar `z-index: 12` and semi-transparent `#f8f8f8` background allowed content bleed-through on mobile |
| [main.scss](cci:7://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/static/pretixcontrol/scss/main.scss:0:0-0:0) | No mobile-specific navbar constraints meant elements overflowed the 50 px bar on narrow screens |

---

## Changes

### 1. [app/eventyay/static/orga/css/_layout.css](cci:7://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/static/orga/css/_layout.css:0:0-0:0)

**Navbar fixes (mobile ≤ 768 px):**
- Added `flex-wrap: nowrap` and `overflow: hidden` to prevent the navbar from
  expanding vertically on small screens.
- `.navbar-brand` now gets `max-width: 45vw` + `text-overflow: ellipsis` so
  long organiser/event names truncate instead of pushing other items off screen.
- `.navbar-collapse` constrained with `flex-basis: 0 / flex-grow: 1 / min-width: 0`.
- `.nav-item.d-none.d-md-block` (username + global-nav-menu) forced hidden with
  `display: none !important` to enforce consistent hiding on mobile.
- `button.admin-mode` / `a.end-admin-mode` compacted (`font-size: 12px`,
  tighter padding) so they fit alongside the brand on narrow screens.

**Sidebar fixes (mobile ≤ 768 px):**
- Changed `position: absolute` → `position: fixed` so the sidebar stays at
  viewport-relative coordinates when the page is scrolled.
- Added explicit solid background `var(--color-grey-ultralight, #f8f8f8)` and
  stronger `box-shadow: 4px 0 12px rgba(0,0,0,0.25)` to prevent bleed-through.
- Added `body:has(aside.sidebar-uncollapsed)::before` — a full-height dim
  backdrop (`rgba(0,0,0,0.35)` at `z-index: 590`) covering the viewport area
  beside the open sidebar, visually separating it from page content.

**Page content fix:**
- Restored explicit `margin-top: 50px` and `height: calc(100vh - 50px)` on
  `#page-content` inside the mobile query, matching what the desktop rule sets.
  Previously the mobile override removed this, causing content to slide beneath
  the fixed navbar.

---

### 2. [app/eventyay/static/pretixcontrol/scss/_sidebar.scss](cci:7://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/static/pretixcontrol/scss/_sidebar.scss:0:0-0:0)

**Mobile sidebar fixes (≤ 767 px):**
- Background changed: `#f8f8f8` → `#ffffff` (guaranteed fully opaque white so
  page content cannot be seen through the sidebar panel).
- `z-index` raised: `12` → `1040` (above Bootstrap dropdowns z=1000 and modal
  backdrops z=1040-range, but below the fixed navbar at z=1100).
- Box-shadow strengthened: `2px 0 5px rgba(0,0,0,0.2)` →
  `4px 0 12px rgba(0,0,0,0.25)` for a more defined sidebar edge.
- Added `body:not(.sidebar-minimized) #wrapper::before` dim backdrop
  (`rgba(0,0,0,0.40)` at `z-index: 1039`) that covers the entire page behind
  the open sidebar, preventing visual overlap between sidebar items and the
  underlying content.

---

### 3. [app/eventyay/static/pretixcontrol/scss/main.scss](cci:7://file:///Users/amreshanand/Desktop/eventyay/app/eventyay/static/pretixcontrol/scss/main.scss:0:0-0:0)

**Mobile navbar fixes (≤ 767 px) — new `@media` block:**
- Navbar locked to `height: 50px` with `flex-wrap: nowrap` and
  `overflow: hidden` to prevent any child forcing a taller bar on mobile.
- `.navbar-brand` constrained with `max-width: 40vw`, `text-overflow: ellipsis`,
  and `flex-shrink: 1` for graceful name truncation.
- `.navbar-top-links.navbar-right` set to `display: flex` with `flex-shrink: 0`
  to keep right-side elements from being displaced.
- `#button-sudo` (Admin mode button): text label hidden on mobile — only the
  `fa-id-card` icon remains, saving ~80 px of horizontal space while keeping
  the action accessible.
- User profile link (`.header-nav`): `font-size: 0` hides the name text;
  `fa-user` icon kept at `font-size: 18px`; caret hidden — clean icon-only
  appearance on mobile.
- `.navbar-toggle-sidebar` margin reset to `0` to prevent vertical
  misalignment in the flex row.

---

## Testing

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Navbar on event start page (mobile) | Items overflow/overlap | Brand truncates, buttons compact, single clean row |
| Sidebar open on mobile (orga area) | Page text bleeds through sidebar | Solid sidebar, dim backdrop, no bleed-through |
| Sidebar open on mobile (pretixcontrol) | z=12 sidebar behind page content | z=1040 sidebar fully overlays content |
| Event page content (orga, mobile) | Title renders behind navbar | `margin-top: 50px` ensures correct offset |
| Desktop (all pages) | ✅ Working correctly | ✅ Unchanged — zero desktop impact |

---

Fixes #2462

prs.title: fix: constrain form label clickable area to visible text only
prs.body:
Summary
On the Widget Settings page, the form labels in the "Widget generation" section (Widget language, Schedule display format, Limit days) have a clickable area that extends far to the left of the visible label text. Clicking anywhere in the empty space to the left unexpectedly triggers focus/opens the associated input or dropdown.

This affects all tabular form layouts across the organizer interface that use the col-md-3 col-form-label pattern.

Root Cause
The <label> element is assigned Bootstrap grid classes col-md-3 col-form-label, which makes it occupy the full 25% column width. The label text is right-aligned via text-align: right, so visually the text appears only on the right side of the column. However, the entire column is a <label> element with a 
for
 attribute, making the full 25% width interactive.
 
 ┌─────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ← clickable empty space → Text │  Input/Dropdown                      │
│         (25% column)            │  (75% column)                        │
└─────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘

Fix
Added two CSS properties to the .col-form-label.col-md-3 selector in 
_forms.css
:

width: fit-content;
margin-left: auto;

width: fit-content — constrains the <label> element to only the width of its text content
margin-left: auto — pushes the label to the right side of the column, preserving the right-aligned layout


┌──────────────────────── ┬──────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐
│   (non-interactive)     │ Text │  Input/Dropdown                      │
│                         │  ↑   │  (75% column)                        │
│                         │ only │                                      │
│                         │click │                                      │
└─────────────────────────┴──────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘

Files Changed
app/eventyay/static/common/css/_forms.css
 — 2 lines added
Testing
Widget Settings page: Labels only trigger input focus when clicked on the visible text
All other settings pages (General, Review, Email): No visual or functional regression — labels remain right-aligned and properly spaced

## Summary by Sourcery

Bug Fixes:
- Limit the interactive area of col-form-label col-md-3 labels so only the text region triggers focus on associated inputs, avoiding unexpected clicks in surrounding empty space.

prs.title: feat: add translation editing support for checkbox fields in CFP flow…
prs.body:
Add Translation Editing Support for Checkbox Fields in CFP Flow Editor
Issue
In the CFP flow editor, fields rendered as checkboxes (e.g., Gravatar, Don't record) cannot have translations added or edited via the standard edit popup. This is inconsistent with other configurable fields such as Track, where the popup allows editing translations for all enabled event languages.

The translation fields already exist in the database — this is a UI-only gap.

Root Cause
In 
cfp_flow.js
, the FieldComponent render method excluded CheckboxInput widgets from the translation editing UI:

javascript
if (this.field.widget !== 'CheckboxInput') {
    // Translation inputs and click-to-edit logic — checkboxes skipped entirely
}
Additionally, the label rendering bypassed labelContent for checkboxes with a static, non-interactive template.

Solution
Updated the FieldComponent in 
cfp_flow.js
 to handle CheckboxInput fields with the same translation editing pattern used for other field types:

Editor view: Checkbox labels are now clickable and open the edit popup
Popup view: Displays the checkbox preview along with translation input fields for all enabled event languages
Label rendering: Unified to always use labelContent, since checkbox handling is now included within it
No backend changes were required — 
_get_step_config_from_data()
 in 
flow.py
 already persists 
label
 as an i18n field for all widget types.

Files Changed
app/eventyay/static/orga/js/cfp_flow.js
Verification
Checkbox fields open the edit popup on click
Translation inputs appear for all enabled event languages
Saved translations persist correctly
No regression for existing translatable fields and non-checkbox field types

## Summary by Sourcery

Enable translation editing for checkbox fields in the CFP flow editor and align their label rendering with other field types.

New Features:
- Allow checkbox fields in the CFP flow editor to be edited via the translation modal for all enabled locales.

Bug Fixes:
- Fix missing UI support for adding and editing translations on checkbox fields while preserving existing i18n persistence.

Enhancements:
- Unify label rendering so all field types, including checkboxes, share the same translation-aware labelContent handling.
- Improve general code formatting and consistency in the CFP flow Vue components without changing behavior.
Fixes #2533

prs.title: fix: handle OSError on Windows when accessing file field paths in Fil…
prs.body:
Fix: OSError [Errno 22] on Windows when saving Review Settings
Problem
Saving review settings on Windows raises OSError [Errno 22] Invalid argument, preventing organizers from updating score categories, review phases, or any review configuration.

Root Cause
When saving review settings, the following call chain is triggered:

ReviewSettingsForm.save()
  → JsonSubfieldMixin.save()
    → Event.save()
      → FileCleanupMixin.save()  ← Error occurs here
FileCleanupMixin.save() iterates over all file fields on the 
Event
 model (
logo
, header_image, 
custom_css
) and accesses the .path property on FieldFile objects to compare old and new file paths:

python
if new_value and old_value.path != new_value.path:
On Windows, FieldFile.path internally calls self.storage.path(self.name), which can raise OSError [Errno 22] Invalid argument if the stored filename contains characters that are invalid on Windows (e.g., colons), or if the OS-level path resolution fails.

This error is triggered even though the review settings page does not involve any file uploads — it happens because Event.save() always passes through FileCleanupMixin.save(), which checks all file fields regardless of what was actually modified.

Fix
Wrapped the .path access in a try-except block that catches OSError and NotImplementedError. When path resolution fails, the file cleanup for that specific field is skipped via continue, allowing the save operation to complete successfully.

python
try:
    old_path = old_value.path
    new_path = new_value.path if new_value else None
except (OSError, NotImplementedError):
    continue
This is a safe approach because:

File cleanup is a best-effort operation — skipping it only means stale files may not be automatically deleted
The existing 
_delete_files()
 method already uses suppress(Exception) for the same reason
Normal behavior on Linux/macOS and valid Windows paths is completely unchanged
Files Changed
app/eventyay/base/models/mixins.py
 — FileCleanupMixin.save()

## Summary by Sourcery

Bug Fixes:
- Prevent OSError and NotImplementedError when resolving file paths during file cleanup, allowing saves to succeed even with invalid or unsupported storage paths.

solve #2541

prs.title: feat: enhance loader with dramatic animated text, split success messa…
prs.body:
feat: enhance loader with dramatic animated text, split success message, and improve system status integration

Loader: Refactored to handle split lines.
Success Message: "ACCESS GRANTED" is now separated, bold, green, and pulsing.

prs.title: fixed Templates
prs.body:
Title: Fix: Add missing template for Change Password view

Description:

This pull request resolves a bug where the "Change Password" view was failing due to a missing template.

Background:

Previously, in PR #1684, the project was configured to use the default django-allauth template for the "Change Password" functionality. However, a subsequent change in PR #1722 updated the ACCOUNT_TEMPLATE_EXTENSION setting to use the .jinja extension for all account-related templates.

This change inadvertently broke the "Change Password" page, as django-allauth was still attempting to locate its default template, which has an .html extension, leading to a TemplateDoesNotExist error.

Solution:

To fix this, I have created a new Jinja template specifically for the "Change Password" view.

New File Added: app/eventyay/eventyay_common/templates/eventyay_common/auth/password_change.jinja
The new template extends our base authentication layout (eventyay_common/auth/base.html) and renders the required form for a user to change their password, ensuring a consistent look and feel with the rest of the application. This resolves the template loading issue and restores the "Change Password" functionality.

## Summary by Sourcery

Add a missing change-password template and align related UI terminology and styling.

New Features:
- Introduce a Jinja template for the Change Password view that integrates with the shared auth layout and form components.

Bug Fixes:
- Restore the Change Password page by providing a Jinja template compatible with the configured ACCOUNT_TEMPLATE_EXTENSION setting.

Enhancements:
- Rename event question labels in the control panel UI to use the term "Custom Fields" for better clarity.
- Add a reusable `.btn-sharp` utility class to support buttons with square corners in the control interface styling.

prs.title: Align custom field button styling in tickets with talk component
prs.body:
Subject: Align Custom Field Action Button Styling with Talk Component

Description:

This pull request addresses and resolves the styling inconsistencies for action buttons within the custom field sections of the "tickets" and "talk" components.

Previously, the buttons in the custom field area of the tickets component featured a black and white design (btn-default), which differed from the solid blue buttons (btn-primary) used in the talk component. This discrepancy created a fragmented user experience.

The following changes have been implemented to unify the design:

Updated Button Styles: In the tickets component (app/eventyay/presale/templates/pretixpresale/event/checkout_questions.html), the "Copy answers" and "Continue" buttons have been updated from btn-default and btn-success to btn-primary to match the solid blue button style.
Unified Terminology: In app/eventyay/control/templates/pretixcontrol/items/questions.html, all instances of "Question(s)" have been changed to "Custom Field(s)" to ensure consistent terminology.
Added Sharp Button Utility: A new utility class, .btn-sharp, has been added to app/eventyay/static/pretixcontrol/scss/_sb-admin-2.scss. This class removes the border-radius from buttons, aligning their appearance with the sharper style of the talk component.
These modifications ensure that the custom field action buttons now have a consistent and visually coherent design across all relevant components, improving the overall user experience.

## Summary by Sourcery

Align custom field management UI and terminology with the talk component for a more consistent experience.

Enhancements:
- Rename the questions management page and labels to use "Custom Field(s)" terminology throughout the control interface.
- Update action buttons in the custom fields page to use primary button styling for visual emphasis and consistency.
- Introduce a reusable .btn-sharp utility class to provide square-corner buttons matching the talk component design.

prs.title: added dark mode theme
prs.body:
📝 Description

This pull request introduces dark theme support for the Permission Site project.
The goal is to improve accessibility and provide a better visual experience for users who prefer dark mode.

🔧 Changes Made

Implemented a dark color palette (background, text, and button styles).

Updated CSS variables for theme switching.

Added toggle button for light/dark mode.

Ensured contrast ratios meet accessibility standards.

Verified consistent layout and responsive design across pages.

🧪 Testing

Manually tested in Chrome, Firefox, and Safari.

Verified that both light and dark themes display correctly.

Checked that theme preference persists across sessions (using local storage).


🧭 Additional Notes

No breaking changes introduced.

Code follows existing project style and naming conventions.

Ready for review and feedback.

✅ Checklist

 Code compiles without errors

 All existing tests pass.

 UI verified for consistency.y

 Follow the style guidelines

prs.title: add navbar
prs.body: The Student Registration form for the attendance management system provides a streamlined and user-friendly interface for new users to create an account by entering essential information such as their full name, email address, username, password, and current status within the institution. With its clean design and intuitive layout, the form is tailored to simplify the onboarding process for students (and potentially staff), ensuring all necessary details are collected efficiently at the first step. A secure password confirmation option helps verify user entries, while the dropdown menu for status selection allows appropriate categorization within the system. This well-organized registration page is an integral part of any educational platform, as it captures and manages vital data for student records, fosters smoother administration, and supports an effective enrollment process for both users and system operator

prs.title: made changes in files
prs.body:
I updated README.md. Summary of changes (concise):

Added "Contents summary" — outlines typical repo directories and purpose.
Added "Getting started (clone)" — HTTPS and SSH git clone commands.
Added "Raise a Pull Request (typical workflow)" — step-by-step branch, commit, push, PR guidance and PR description checklist.
Added "Local preview and testing" — instructions to check CONTRIBUTING.md and example commands for running a local preview.
Expanded "Contributing & support" — brief contribution guidelines.
Added "Useful links" — links to repo, live docs, issues, templates, discussions, and local paths.
Added "Development details (local setup)" — example local setup for Hugo, MkDocs, and Docker.
Added "Branch & commit conventions" — branch naming and commit message guidance (Conventional Commits example).
Added "CI, linters & checks" — note about CI pipelines and pre-PR checks.
Added "Reporting security issues" — guidance to avoid public issues and reference to GitHub security advisories.
Added "License" — pointer to the LICENSE file.

prs.title: made changes in readme.md
prs.body: To contribute to this project, the following steps were completed: First, the repository was forked to a personal GitHub account, and then cloned locally for editing. Changes were carefully made in a newly created branch to ensure that the original codebase remained intact. Once the modifications were finalized, all changes were staged, committed, and pushed back to the forked repository. Afterwards, a pull request was initiated from the updated branch on GitHub, where a detailed description of the changes was provided for the repository maintainers to review. Throughout the process, attention was paid to clearly communicating the updates and following best practices for collaboration, ensuring the pull request could be easily reviewed and, if approved, merged into the main project.

prs.title: styled the website
prs.body:
I updated both aboutus.html and contactus.html to use a new cohesive styles.css, removed reliance on missing Bootstrap files, and added alt text to images.
I rebuilt styles.css with a classy dark theme, refined typography, elevated cards/panels, modern buttons, improved forms, custom grid utilities to match the existing markup, and subtle gradients/shadows for a premium feel.
Navigation, hero, breadcrumbs, tables, forms, and footer now have consistent, polished styling with responsive tweaks.

prs.title: changes css file
prs.body: The stylesheet adds an Open Sans import and CSS variables for spacing, card height and colors; applies a global box‑sizing reset and baseline typography; defines two body rules (a gradient + then a second rule that overrides it with a full‑cover background image sources/bg1.jpg); styles the header for centered white text; turns .container into a wrapping flex gallery with gaps and centered alignment; makes all images behave like uniform “cards” (fixed width/height, object-fit: cover, rounded corners, subtle border and shadow) with a smooth lift/scale hover effect; includes responsive breakpoints to change columns and image heights on smaller screens; styles the footer with a soft gradient, rounded container, readable text and an overflowed small block; and adds a small utility so Bootstrap spinners sit neatly

I refined nav interactions, headings, and body text for better readability and polish.

prs.title: make the website fully functionable
prs.body: I have developed a fully functional website that includes various interactive and visually appealing features. The website is built using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to ensure a clean structure, attractive design, and smooth functionality. It contains an “About” section that provides detailed information about the individual or organization, along with multiple images to make the website more engaging. A contact form has also been added, allowing visitors to send messages directly through the website. To enhance the visual design, I created a separate style.css file where I made layout adjustments, added hover effects, and improved the overall appearance of the site. In addition, I modified the JavaScript file to add dynamic interactivity, including a Dark Mode and Light Mode feature that allows users to switch between themes for a personalized experience. Overall, the website is responsive, user-friendly, and offers a modern look with smooth transitions and interactive functionality.

prs.title: make the website more classy
prs.body: I have designed a webpage that includes various key elements to enhance user engagement and provide essential information. The page features informative text sections (pText) to communicate the purpose and content clearly, along with relevant images to create a visually appealing layout. A structured footer has been added at the bottom to include links, contact details, or other useful information. There is also a form that allows users to apply for services or opportunities directly through the site. In addition, the page includes a contact section for inquiries, an about section that shares background information, and embedded videos to support the content and provide a more interactive experience.

prs.title: add background color and style the webpage
prs.body: I redesigned this simple calculator webpage to give it a clean, modern, and more realistic look. I added a dark gradient background to make the interface stand out and feel more polished. The calculator layout is structured in a grid, and I styled the buttons with vibrant colors for each function — using blue for addition, purple for subtraction, teal for multiplication, green for division and equals, and red for clear. Each button has a 3D, soft-shadow effect to give it a tactile, clickable appearance. The display area at the top clearly shows the current input or result, styled to match the overall dark theme. The typography and spacing are carefully adjusted to enhance readability and usability. Overall, this redesign focuses on visual clarity, responsive layout, and giving the calculator a professional, app-like appearance.

prs.title: make the website classy
prs.body: I created this HTML webpage as a beginner-friendly guide, focusing on both content and design. I used a dark gradient background along with a subtle background image to give it a modern look. All content is neatly organized inside rounded boxes to improve readability and layout. The heading is styled to look classy and bold, placed with an avatar at the top to make the page more welcoming. Each section—like HTML basics, tips, and a cheat sheet—is clearly separated and easy to follow. Overall, the page is clean, structured, and designed to make learning HTML simple and visually engaging.

prs.title: make the webpage more  classy
prs.body:
I created this webpage as a simple and friendly introduction to HTML, aimed at beginners who are just starting their web development journey. The design is clean and centered, with a dark gradient background to keep the focus on the content while giving it a modern look. At the top, I added a fun avatar and a welcome message to make the page feel more personal and approachable. I started by explaining the three most basic HTML elements—, , and —with short, clear descriptions. To help users go beyond the basics, I included a "More About HTML" section that covers additional elements like headings, images, and lists, along with a quick note about the importance of semantic structure and accessibility. I also highlighted a key tip—that HTML is not case sensitive—since it’s a common mistake for beginners. Then, I added a few quick HTML tips on structuring pages, using CSS for styling, and making layouts responsive. Finally, I created a small HTML cheat sheet at the bottom with commonly used tags like

,

,

    , and , so learners can refer to it easily. Overall, my goal was to keep everything simple, well-organized, and visually clear to make learning HTML less intimidating and more enjoyable.
